front: Figure: a Caribbean-born Founding Father who served as George Washington's Revolutionary War aide-de-camp before becoming the first US Secretary of the Treasury
Work: series of 85 essays defending the Constitution under the pseudonym "Publius"; 51 were written by this Founding Father
Figure: after George Washington's death, he succeeded him as Senior Officer of the US Army and suggested using it to invade Spanish colonies
Place: island where Hamilton was born, before he was raised on St. Croix in the Danish Virgin Islands
Figure: upon arriving in New York City, he lived with the Patriot spy Hercules Mulligan and attended Columbia University
Figure: exchanged flirty letters with fellow Continental Army officer John Laurens, leading some historians to speculate he may have been bisexual
Figure: Hamilton's father-in-law, a Revolutionary War general accused by Horatio Gates of dereliction of duty, who later served as a New York senator before losing his seat to Aaron Burr
Figure: even before the Reynolds Affair, Hamilton was a frequent target of the National Gazette, a newspaper edited by this early American author
Figure: John Adams, with whom he battled for control of the Federalist Party, called him a "bastard brat of a Scotch pedlar"
Figure: his first published works refuted the loyalist Westchester Farmer Samuel Seabury
work: The Federalist Papers and Constitutional Theory

front: Work: numbered Federalist Paper, written by Hamilton, that argued against the need for a Bill of Rights
work: The Federalist Papers and Constitutional Theory

front: Concept: constitutional clause Hamilton interpreted broadly, claiming it granted Congress plenary power to spend
work: The Federalist Papers and Constitutional Theory

front: Event: pseudonymous debate between Hamilton and Madison over the constitutionality of Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality
work: The Federalist Papers and Constitutional Theory

front: Concept: policy Hamilton pushed for as part of an economic program that also included chartering the First Bank of the United States; agreed to as part of the "Compromise of 1790" in exchange for locating the capital in Washington, D.C.
Work: Treasury report, along with the Report on Manufactures, written by Hamilton
Concept: Hamilton's scheme to bolster national credit by taking in old bonds and issuing new ones matching their face value
Institution: early political party led by Hamilton whose platform included a national bank and good relations with Britain
Institution: New York newspaper founded by Hamilton, later edited by William Cullen Bryant and Carl Schurz
Figure: wartime Superintendent of Finance who turned down the first Treasury Secretary post and recommended Hamilton instead
Figure: woman with whom Hamilton had an extramarital affair; he paid blackmail money to her husband to keep it quiet
Figure: pamphleteer who exposed the Reynolds Affair to the public, and who later exposed Thomas Jefferson's affair with Sally Hemings
Work: 95-page pamphlet in which Hamilton admitted to and apologized for the Reynolds Affair while refuting separate corruption charges uncovered with help from Speaker Frederick Muhlenberg
Institution: bank Hamilton founded, under the guise of establishing a water system, to compete with Aaron Burr's Bank of New York
Figure: Federalist congressman Hamilton convinced to abstain from voting in the contested 1800 election to prevent Aaron Burr from becoming president

Event: 1804 encounter in Weehawken, New Jersey in which Vice President Aaron Burr fatally shot Hamilton; Burr's second William Van Ness hid his face with an umbrella from witnesses including David Hosack
Figure: former army officer Aaron Burr was later charged with (and acquitted of) treason for allegedly conspiring with him to form an empire in the western United States
Work: Lin-Manuel Miranda musical about Hamilton, originally an Off-Broadway show whose song "Burn" depicts Eliza destroying Hamilton's love letters

Work: Hamilton's love letters to Eliza Schuyler, part of a collection curated by Harold Syrett, underwent 2018 hyperspectral analysis revealing a crossed-out racy passage about "unrestrained intercourses"
back: Alexander Hamilton's love letters to Eliza Schuyler

front: Figure: actor who co-wrote an early play about Hamilton with Mary P. Hamlin and later portrayed him in a 1931 film
Figure: Scottish nobleman, James Hamilton, 4th Duke of Hamilton, was described in one bonus as a distant relative of Alexander Hamilton; he also died in a pistol duel, at Hyde Park in 1712
